quickInsight: God doesn't need your perfection — He needs your willing heart.

quotableVerses.text: Ye shall be holy: for I the Lord your God am holy.
Whosoever is of a willing heart, let him bring it, an offering of the Lord.
Ye shall offer for a sacrifice unto me a broken heart and a contrite spirit.
I am able to make you holy.

sections.title: The Story
items: God tells the Israelites to build a tabernacle — a portable temple — right in the middle of the desert. Not when life is easy. Right now.
The people don't just obey — they bring so much gold, fabric, and wood that Moses has to tell them to STOP giving (Exodus 36:6–7). They overwhelm the project with generosity.
God fills specific people with skill and creativity to do the work — Bezaleel and Aholiab didn't just have willing hearts, God gave them the talent to match (Exodus 36:1).
When it's finished, the glory of the Lord fills the tabernacle so powerfully that even Moses can't enter (Exodus 40:34–35). God shows up when His people build something holy.

sections.title: Why This Matters to You
items: "Be holy" sounds impossible when you messed up yesterday and you'll probably mess up tomorrow. But God didn't say "be perfect first, then come to me." He said build Me a place in your life and I'll show up.
The Israelites had just been slaves. They didn't have it all together. God asked them to bring what they had — not what they didn't have. He's asking you the same thing.

You might feel like you don't have much to offer — you're 14, you're figuring life out. But God gave Bezaleel specific skills and said "use THOSE." Your thing — whether it's humor, loyalty, or just showing up — that's your offering.
Holiness isn't a personality type. It's a direction. Every time you choose the Savior over the easier path, you're building your own tabernacle.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: The Israelites gave so much Moses had to say stop. When's the last time you went all-in on something — not because you had to, but because you actually wanted to?
God gave Bezaleel artistic talent specifically to build the tabernacle. What's something you're good at that you've never thought of as a way to serve God?
Leviticus 19 has a bunch of specific commandments — don't steal, don't lie, don't hold grudges, be fair. Which one of those is hardest in your friend group right now?
The Savior says He can make us holy (D&C 60:7). What does that change about how you see your own mistakes?

sections.title: How to Teach It
items: Empty your pockets challenge: Have each boy empty his pockets or backpack. Whatever they've got — that's their offering. The Israelites didn't go shopping; they brought what they already had. Ask: What has God already given YOU that He might be asking you to use?

Draw the tabernacle layout on a whiteboard. Each item pointed to Christ — the altar (His sacrifice), the laver/water (baptism/cleansing), the bread (sacrament), the light (He is the Light). Ask them: if your life were a temple, what would people find inside?
Read D&C 60:7 — "I am able to make you holy." Emphasize the word "I." Not you. Him. Holiness isn't a solo project. It's what happens when you let the Savior in.

sections.title: Do This at Mutual
items: Service build night: Pick something the ward or a neighbor needs — assemble hygiene kits, build a shelf, prep a meal. Connect it back to Exodus 35: God asked for willing hands, and these boys have them. Let them see their skills matter.

Temple prep night: Look at photos or a virtual tour of the nearest temple. Point out things that echo the tabernacle — altars, white clothing, sacred spaces. Talk about what it means that God still asks us to build holy places.

sections.title: Restoration Connection
items: The tabernacle was a portable temple — and we still build temples today for the same reason: sacred ordinances that make covenants real. Washing, anointing, and clothing in holy garments (Exodus 40:12–14) happen in our temples right now.
3 Nephi 9:19–20 — the Savior Himself retired animal sacrifice and asked for a broken heart and contrite spirit instead. The sacrifice didn't end; it got personal.
President Eyring taught that the plan of happiness IS the plan of holiness — they're the same thing. Becoming holy isn't losing your personality; it's becoming who you were actually meant to be.

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Pick one thing from Leviticus 19 — don't hold grudges, be honest, look out for someone who's struggling — and actually do it this week. One specific thing, one specific person.

Before you take the sacrament next Sunday, think about one thing you're offering to the Lord this week — your time, your attitude, your effort. Bring it like the Israelites brought their gold: willingly.
